insights: Breadth: 1/16 stocks up — broadly weak today
context: Broad selling today — most large-caps fell. Down days are exactly when disciplined SIP investors keep buying units cheaper.
Money rotated into FMCG and out of Realty. Sector leadership shifts often — chasing last month's hottest sector is a classic beginner trap.
India VIX is low (13.29) — calm, low expected volatility. Calm markets breed complacency; a good time to check your asset allocation, not to chase risk.

gk.t: Antarctica is the largest desert on Earth
gk.teaser: A desert is about dryness, not heat.
gk.more: A desert is defined by how little precipitation it receives, not by temperature. By that measure Antarctica — vast, frozen and extremely dry — is the world's largest desert, bigger than the Sahara. Parts of its interior have seen no rain or snow for millions of years, making them some of the driest places on the planet.

gk.t: India has 28 states and 8 union territories
gk.teaser: States have their own governments; UTs are run by the centre.
gk.more: The newest state is Telangana (2014, carved from Andhra Pradesh). Delhi and Puducherry are special UTs with their own assemblies. Jammu & Kashmir became a UT in 2019. Largest state by area: Rajasthan; by population: Uttar Pradesh (~240 million — more people than most countries).

gk.teaser: Jana Gana Mana, written by Rabindranath Tagore.
gk.more: Tagore wrote it in Bengali in 1911; it was adopted as the anthem in 1950. He also wrote Bangladesh's anthem — the only person whose songs are two countries' anthems. Vande Mataram (by Bankim Chandra Chatterjee) is the national song, which has equal status but no time rule.

gk.teaser: Asia is the largest continent; the Pacific the largest ocean.
gk.more: Order by size: Asia, Africa, North America, South America, Antarctica, Europe, Australia. The five oceans: Pacific, Atlantic, Indian, Southern, Arctic. The Pacific alone is bigger than all land on Earth combined.

gk.t: Light takes ~8 minutes from the Sun to Earth
gk.teaser: When you see the Sun, you see it 8 minutes in the past.
gk.more: Light travels ~300,000 km per second; the Sun is ~150 million km away. From the Moon, light takes just 1.3 seconds. The nearest star beyond the Sun, Proxima Centauri, is 4.2 light YEARS away — meaning you see it as it was 4 years ago.

gk.t: The Indian rupee symbol ₹ arrived only in 2010
gk.teaser: Designed by D. Udaya Kumar, blending Devanagari 'Ra' and Latin 'R'.
gk.more: Before 2010 India wrote 'Rs.' like several other countries. The horizontal lines represent the tricolour and the equality sign — a nod to economic balance. Only a handful of currencies have unique symbols: $, €, £, ¥, ₹.

gk.t: Mount Everest grows ~4 mm every year
gk.teaser: At 8,849 m it's the world's highest point — and still rising.
gk.more: The India-Eurasia plate collision keeps pushing it up. Its official height was revised in 2020 jointly by Nepal and China. The first confirmed summit: Edmund Hillary and Tenzing Norgay, 29 May 1953.

gk.t: India's independence and republic days differ by purpose
gk.teaser: 15 Aug 1947 = freedom; 26 Jan 1950 = our own Constitution.
gk.more: Independence Day marks the end of British rule. Republic Day marks the Constitution coming into force, making India a republic with its own head of state instead of the British monarch. That's why Republic Day has the grand parade — it celebrates the state India built for itself.

gk.t: A cheque and a demand draft are not the same
gk.teaser: A cheque can bounce; a DD is prepaid so it cannot.
gk.more: A cheque instructs YOUR bank to pay and fails if your balance is short. A demand draft is bought from the bank upfront — the bank itself guarantees payment. That's why institutions ask for DDs for fees. Both are fading as UPI handles 80%+ of India's retail digital payments.

gk.t: The Olympics happen every 4 years — mostly
gk.teaser: Summer and Winter Games alternate every 2 years.
gk.more: Cancelled only by the World Wars (1916, 1940, 1944) and delayed once by COVID (Tokyo 2020 held in 2021). India's first Olympic medal as an independent nation: hockey gold, 1948 London — beating Britain in Britain a year after independence.

gkMore.teaser: Money earning money on the money it already earned.
gkMore.more: ₹1 lakh at 12% becomes ₹3.1 lakh in 10 years and ₹9.6 lakh in 20 — the growth accelerates because each year's interest joins the principal. The 'rule of 72': divide 72 by the interest rate to estimate doubling time. At 12%, money doubles every ~6 years.

gkMore.t: India defined zero as a number
gkMore.teaser: Not just a placeholder for nothing — a number you can calculate with.
gkMore.more: Many cultures used a symbol for 'nothing,' but the 7th-century mathematician Brahmagupta was the first to treat zero as a number in its own right, writing rules for adding, subtracting and multiplying with it. Combined with the Indian decimal place-value system, this idea travelled through the Arab world to Europe and became the foundation of modern arithmetic. It is why we can write 1,000,000 without inventing a new symbol for every power of ten.

gkMore.t: India has the world's longest written constitution
gkMore.teaser: It began with 395 articles — and keeps growing.
gkMore.more: When it came into force on 26 January 1950, the Constitution of India had 395 articles, 22 parts and 8 schedules, making it the lengthiest national constitution ever written. Its framers borrowed selectively from abroad — the parliamentary system from Britain, fundamental rights from the US, directive principles from Ireland. Decades of amendments have since pushed it well past 470 articles and 12 schedules.

gkMore.t: Drafting India's constitution took nearly three years
gkMore.teaser: 2 years, 11 months and 18 days of debate.
gkMore.more: The Constituent Assembly, chaired by Rajendra Prasad with B. R. Ambedkar heading the drafting committee, worked from December 1946 to November 1949 — 2 years, 11 months and 18 days. The final document was not printed but written out by hand in flowing calligraphy by Prem Behari Narain Raizada, and illustrated by artists from Shantiniketan. Members debated it almost line by line.

gkMore.t: Sikkim was an independent kingdom until 1975
gkMore.teaser: India's 22nd state was once ruled by a monarch.
gkMore.more: For centuries Sikkim was ruled by the Namgyal dynasty of Chogyals. After a 1975 referendum abolished the monarchy, it merged with India to become the 22nd state. Today it is India's least populous state and, in 2016, became the country's first fully organic-farming state.

gkMore.t: The shortest war in history lasted under 40 minutes
gkMore.teaser: Britain versus Zanzibar, 1896 — over before lunch.
gkMore.more: On 27 August 1896, Britain bombarded the Sultan's palace in Zanzibar after a disputed succession. Resistance collapsed in roughly 38 to 45 minutes, making it the shortest recorded war in history. Around 500 of the Sultan's defenders were killed or wounded; the only British casualty was a single injured sailor who later recovered.

gkMore.t: Cleopatra lived closer to the Moon landing than to the pyramids
gkMore.teaser: The Great Pyramid was already ancient in her lifetime.
gkMore.more: The Great Pyramid of Giza was built around 2560 BCE. Cleopatra died in 30 BCE — over 2,500 years later. The 1969 Moon landing was under 2,000 years after her death, so she lived chronologically nearer to Apollo 11 than to the pyramid-builders. Ancient Egypt spanned so much time that 'ancient' meant something even to the ancients.

gkMore.t: Oxford University is older than the Aztec Empire
gkMore.teaser: Lectures were running before Tenochtitlan existed.
gkMore.more: Teaching at Oxford dates back to at least 1096. The Aztec capital Tenochtitlan was founded around 1325, more than two centuries later. So when Aztec priests were laying the first stones of their island city, English students had already been studying at Oxford for generations.

gkMore.teaser: Archaeologists have found edible honey thousands of years old.
gkMore.more: Honey's very low moisture and high acidity make it hostile to bacteria, and bees add an enzyme that releases tiny amounts of hydrogen peroxide. Sealed properly, it can last practically forever. Pots of honey recovered from ancient Egyptian tombs were reportedly still preserved after millennia.

gkMore.teaser: Thank the potassium — but you'd need millions to worry.
gkMore.more: Bananas are rich in potassium, a small fraction of which is the naturally radioactive isotope potassium-40. The dose is so tiny that scientists half-jokingly measure trivial radiation exposure in 'banana equivalent doses.' You would have to eat millions in one sitting for it to be dangerous — and your own body already contains radioactive potassium.

learn.title: A/B testing in five sentences
learn.teaser: Split users randomly, change one thing, measure the difference.
body: Random assignment makes the two groups statistically identical except for your change, so any outcome gap is caused by it — this is the cleanest causation tool in product work. The catch is sample size: small tests 'win' by luck constantly.

Standard practice: decide the metric and minimum effect before launching, run a significance check (p < 0.05 convention), and beware peeking early — checking daily and stopping at the first 'significant' result inflates false positives dramatically.

learn.title: Why the Sensex is called the Sensex
learn.teaser: 'Sensitive Index' — 30 big BSE stocks standing in for the market.
body: It tracks 30 of the largest, most-traded companies on the Bombay Stock Exchange, Asia's oldest (1875). Base value: 100 in 1978–79 — so ~77,000 means those stocks are worth roughly 770× their 1979 level. The Nifty 50 is the NSE's 50-stock equivalent.
Index composition changes over time — laggards get dropped, risers added — which builds in survivorship: the index isn't the same 30 companies your grandfather's Sensex had.

learn.title: What the repo rate actually does
learn.teaser: RBI's lending rate to banks — the master dial for every EMI.
body: Banks borrow from RBI at the repo rate. Raise it and all credit gets costlier, cooling spending and inflation; cut it and money cheapens, boosting growth. The Monetary Policy Committee resets it every two months against a 4% (±2%) CPI target.

Transmission is the catch: banks pass rate cuts to depositors fast and to borrowers slowly. That lag — and how much of a cut actually reaches your home loan — is what analysts fight about after every MPC meeting.

learn.title: What GDP measures — and misses
learn.teaser: Everything produced in a year; nothing about who gets it.
body: GDP is the market value of final goods and services produced in a period. India's ~$4 trillion ranks among the world's largest — but per capita it ranks far lower, divided across 1.4 billion people.
It ignores unpaid work, inequality and environmental damage, and a hospital bill raises GDP the same as a festival. '7% growth' means output grew — not that everyone is 7% better off.

learn.title: How India measures inflation
learn.teaser: CPI prices a fixed basket a typical household buys.
body: Food is nearly half of India's CPI basket, so vegetable prices swing the headline number. '6% inflation' means the basket costs 6% more than a year ago. RBI's legal mandate: keep it at 4%, within 2–6%.

WPI tracks wholesale prices businesses pay each other; the two can diverge sharply. For personal finance, your true inflation depends on your basket — education and health have outpaced CPI for years.

learn.title: What an LLM actually is
learn.teaser: A next-word predictor trained on most of the written internet.
body: Models like GPT, Gemini and Claude predict the next token; at sufficient scale that absorbs grammar, facts, reasoning patterns and code. Chat behaviour comes from fine-tuning with human feedback afterward.

Two limits follow: hallucination (fluent but wrong — they optimise plausibility, not truth) and frozen knowledge (they know training data, not today) unless given search or documents. Every 'AI made this up' story traces to the first; every 'AI doesn't know X' to the second.

learn.title: Why oil moves everything
learn.teaser: A fifth of world supply ships through one strait near Iran.
body: Crude feeds transport, plastics, fertiliser and power — an oil spike is a tax on the whole economy. The Strait of Hormuz chokepoint is why Gulf tensions instantly lift Brent.

India imports 85%+ of its crude: costlier oil widens the trade deficit, weakens the rupee, and lifts CPI. That chain — Gulf headline → rupee → your petrol pump → RBI's next rate call — is worth internalising.

learn.title: Bulls, bears, and the names
learn.teaser: Horns toss up; paws swipe down.
body: Bull market: sustained rise (+20% from a low). Bear: sustained fall (−20% from a high). The likely origin is the animals' attack styles, plus an old proverb about selling the bearskin before catching the bear — betting on a fall.
March 2020's COVID crash was a three-month bear phase; the recovery after was a textbook bull run. 'Bullish on X' simply means expecting X to rise.
